concepts.title: Gibbs free energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Free Energies

Gibbs free energy $\text{G}$, is defined as $\text{G}=\text{H}-\text{TS}$

where $\text{H}$ is the enthalpy, $\text{T}$ is the temperature in Kelvin scale, $\text{S}$ is the entropy. TS is the part of system’s energy that is already disordered and H $-\text{TS}(=\text{G})$ is the part of the system’s energy and that is still ordered hence, free (available) to cause spontaneous change by becoming disordered.

  • $\text{G}$ is also a state function hence, change in free energy $(\Delta \text{G})$ for a process is independent of path
  • $\Delta \text{G }=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}$

Also $\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{total }}}\text{ }=\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{system }}}+\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{surroundings }}}$

$=\Delta \text{S}-\frac{\Delta \text{H}}{\text{T}}$

Since, $\Delta {{S}_{\text{surroundings }}}=-\frac{\Delta H}{T}$

(we write \(\Delta {{S}_{(sustem)}}\) simply as \(\Delta S\))

$\text{T}\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{total }}}\text{ }=\text{T}\Delta \text{S}-\Delta \text{H }$

or \(-\text{T}\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{total }}}\text{ }=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}\)

\(-\text{T}\Delta {{\text{S}}_{\text{total }}}\text{ }=\Delta \text{G}\)

Thus, $\Delta \mathbf{G}$ and $\Delta {{\mathbf{S}}_{\text{total }}}$ have opposite signs ($\text{T}$ is always positive); hence sign of $\Delta \text{G}$ can also be used to predict feasibility of a reaction at constant temperature and pressure. For a reaction to be spontaneous if $\Delta \text{S}>0$ then $\Delta \text{G}<0$ non spontaneous if $\Delta \text{S}<0$ then $\Delta \text{G}>0$ in equilibrium if $\Delta \text{S}=0$ then $\Delta \text{G}=0$

$\Delta \text{G}=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} =\Delta \text{E}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S} & \left( \because \Delta \text{S}=\frac{\text{q}}{\text{T}} \right) \\ =\Delta \text{E}-\text{q}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}\quad \Delta \text{E}=\text{q}-\text{W by first law } & {} \\ \end{array}$

$\Delta \text{G }=-\text{W}+\text{P}\Delta \text{V}$

$-\Delta \text{G }=\text{W}-\text{P}\Delta \text{V}={{\text{W}}_{\text{net }}}$

$-\Delta \text{G }={{\text{W}}_{\text{net }}}=2.303\text{nRT}\log \frac{{{\text{V}}_{2}}}{~{{\text{V}}_{1}}}$

Thus, decrease in $\text{G},(-\Delta \text{G})$, is equal to net work done = (total work - mechanical work)

Table : Signs of Enthalpy, Entropy and Free Energy Changes and Reaction Spontaneity at Constant Temperature and Pressure 

For a reaction in which a compound in its standard state is formed from its elements in their standard states, the free energy change is the standard free energy of formation, $\Delta {{\mathbf{G}}_{f}}$

$\Delta {{\text{G}}_{\text{f}}}=0$ for the elements,

$\Delta {{\text{G}}_{\text{f}}}\text{ }=\left( \Delta {{\text{G}}_{\text{products }}}-\Delta {{\text{G}}_{\text{reactants }}} \right)$

$=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}$

  • $\Delta \text{G}$is an extensive property,
  • $\Delta \mathbf{G}$changes sign when a process is reversed.

concepts.title: Energy and EMF of cell
concepts.conceptBody:
Energy and Electromotive Force (EMF) of a cell

$\text{Using}$ $\Delta \text{G }=\Delta \text{G}+2.303\text{RT}\log \text{Q}$

$\Delta \text{G }=-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$

$\Delta \text{G}=-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}$

We have $-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=-\text{nF}{{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}+2.303\text{RT}\log \text{Q}$

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}-\frac{2.303\text{RT}}{\text{nF}}\log \text{Q}$

This is called Nernst equation for emf of a cell.

${{\text{E}}_{\text{cell}}}$ is the emf of a cell in a given state, $\text{E}$ cell is the standard emf, $\text{n}$ the number of electrons exchanged, $\text{F}$ the faraday of electricity, $\text{R}$ gas constant and $\text{T}$ the temperature. At $298~\text{K}$

$\frac{2.303\text{RT}}{\text{F}}\text{ }=\frac{2.303\times 8.314\times 298}{96500}$

$=0.0591~\text{V}$

$\therefore \quad {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}={{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}-\frac{0.0591}{\text{n}}\log \text{Q}$

When equilibrium is attained, $\quad {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=0$ and $\text{Q}=\text{K}$

$\therefore \quad {{\text{E}}_{\text{cell }}}=\frac{00591}{\text{n}}\log \text{K}$

concepts.title: Heat of reaction
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat of Reaction

It is defined as the “amount of heat evolved or absorbed when the reacting species, as represented by a balanced chemical equation have completely reacted.”

$\text{A}+\text{B}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}+\text{D},\Delta \text{H}=\text{q}$

$\Delta \text{H }={{\text{H}}_{\text{Products }}}-{{\text{H}}_{\text{Reactants }}}$

$=\left( {{\text{H}}_{\text{C}}}+{{\text{H}}_{\text{D}}} \right)-\left( {{\text{H}}_{\text{A}}}+{{\text{H}}_{\text{B}}} \right)$

  •  
    • If ${{\text{H}}_{\text{Products }}}={{\text{H}}_{\text{Reactants }}},\Delta \text{H}=0$
    • If ${{\text{H}}_{\text{Products }}}>{{\text{H}}_{\text{Reactants }}},\Delta \text{H}=+\text{ ve,}$ reaction is said to be endothermic.
    • If ${{\text{H}}_{\text{Products }}}<{{\text{H}}_{\text{Reactants }}},\Delta \text{H}=-\text{ve}$, reaction is said to be exothermic.
    • Enthalpy of every element in any state = Zero.

concepts.title: Gibb's free energy and Spontaneity
concepts.conceptBody:
Spontaneity

A spontaneous or natural process is a process that occurs in a system left to itself once started; no action from outside the system (external action) is necessary to make the process continue. On the other hand, a nonspontaneous process will not occur unless some external action is continuously applied. We say that the rusting of iron is spontaneous.

$2\text{F}{{\text{e}}_{(\text{s})}}+3{{\text{O}}_{2(~\text{g})}}\to \text{F}{{\text{e}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3(~\text{s})}}$

Reverse reaction is not impossible, but it is certainly non spontaneous.

Gibbs Free Energy* Change and Spontaneity:

The Gibbs free energy 'G ' for system is defined as

$\text{G}=\text{H}-\text{TS}$

and, for a change at constant $\text{T}$,

$\Delta \text{G}=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S}$

Where $\Delta \text{G}=$ change in Gibbs free energy $\text{G}$

$\Delta \text{H}=$ change in enthalpy $\text{H}$

$\Delta \text{S}=$ change in entropy $\text{S}$

Sign of $\Delta G$ is taken as the true criterion for spontaneity.

For a process occurring at constant $\text{T}$ and $\text{P}$, if

(i) $\Delta \text{G}<0$ (negative) the process is spontaneous

(ii) $\Delta \text{G}>0$ (positive) the process is nonspontaneous

(iii) $\Delta \text{G}=0$ (zero) the process is at equilibrium

Sign of $\Delta \text{G}$, based on eqn. $(\Delta \text{G}=\Delta \text{H}-\text{T}\Delta \text{S})$ can be decided depending on sign of $\Delta \text{H}$ and $\Delta \text{S}$, and magnitude of $\text{T}$, and is explained in Table below.

concepts.title: Relationship of G to Equlibrium constant K
concepts.conceptBody:
Relationship of $\mathbf{\Delta G}$ to the Equilibrium Constant K:

$\Delta \text{G}$ for a reaction under any set of conditions is related to its value for standard conditions, that is $\Delta \text{G}$ by eqn.

$\Delta G=\Delta G+2.303RT\log Q$

under equilibrium condition $\text{Q}={{\text{K}}_{\text{p}}}\text{ }={{\text{K}}_{\text{c}}}=\text{K}$

$\Delta \text{G }=0$

$\Delta \text{G }=-2.303\text{RT}\log \text{K}$

We have replaced ${{K}_{p}}$ or ${{K}_{c}}$ by $K$ called thermodynamic equilibrium constant. For a general reaction

$\text{aA}+\text{bB}\rightleftharpoons \text{cC}+\text{dD}$

$\text{K}=\frac{{{(\text{aC})}^{\text{c}}}{{(\text{aD})}^{4}}}{{{(\text{aA})}^{\text{a}}}{{(\text{aB})}^{\text{b}}}}$

the symbol a represents the activity of the reactants and products. It is actually a ratio of the equilibrium activity of a substance to its activity in its standard state. Thus thermodynamic equilibrium constant is unitless quantity, a being also unitless.

- For pure solids and liquids:$\quad $ a $=1$

- For gases: ideal behaviour is considered and the activity of a gas is equal to its pressure in atm.

- For components in solution: $\quad \text{a}=$ molar concentration

concepts.title: Effect of temprature on enthalpy
concepts.conceptBody:
Effect of temprature on enthalpy

Enthalpy of reaction also depends on temperature at which the reaction is carried out. This is due to variation in the heat capacity of the system with temperature.

Relation between $\Delta \text{H}$ or $\Delta \text{E}$ and temperature are given by Kirchoff’s equation.

$\left( \Delta {{\text{H}}_{2}}-\Delta {{\text{H}}_{1}} \right)=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)$

$\left( \Delta {{\text{E}}_{2}}-\Delta {{\text{E}}_{1}} \right)=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)$

The above relations may be derived as follows:

For a reaction at constant pressure, $\Delta \text{H}={{\text{H}}_{\text{P}}}-{{\text{H}}_{\text{R}}}$

Differentiating it w.r.t. $\text{T}$ at constant $\text{P}$, we get

${{\left( \frac{\text{d}\Delta \text{H}}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{P}}}={{\left( \frac{\text{d}{{\text{H}}_{\text{P}}}}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{P}}}-{{\left( \frac{\text{d}{{\text{H}}_{\text{R}}}}{\text{dT}} \right)}_{\text{P}}}={{\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}} \right)}_{\text{P}}}-{{\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}} \right)}_{\text{R}}}=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}$

or $d(\Delta H)=\Delta {{C}_{P}}dT$

Integrating this equation within appropriate limits, we get

$\int_{{{T}_{1}}}^{{{T}_{2}}}{~d}(\Delta H)=\Delta {{C}_{P}}\int_{{{T}_{1}}}^{{{T}_{2}}}{~d}T$

$\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}}-\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}}=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)$

$\frac{\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}}-\Delta {{\text{H}}_{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}}}{\left( ~{{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)}=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}$ … (1)

This equation is used to find heat of reaction at a temperature when it is known at another temperature.

Similarly, $\frac{\Delta {{\text{E}}_{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}}-\Delta {{\text{E}}_{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}}}{\left( ~{{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)}=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{v}}}$ … (2)

In the limits as $\left( {{\text{T}}_{2}}-{{\text{T}}_{1}} \right)\xrightarrow{{}}0$, equation (1) yields the differential form $\frac{\text{d}(\Delta \text{H})}{\text{dT}}=\Delta {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}$

This shows that the rate of change of the enthalpy of a reaction is equal to the difference in heat capacities of products and reactants.

Since actually the heat capacities themselves vary with temperature, it is sufficiently accurate to use the average value of the heat capacity over the range of temperature considered.
